cities.name: Barrington
cities.intro:
Barrington spans the line between developed Lake County and the moraines of far western suburban Chicago — a mix of equestrian estates north of Cuba Road, established subdivisions south of Route 14, and rural wooded lots throughout. The land here was shaped by the last glaciation, which left kettle holes, mature oak savanna, and patchy heavy clay over deep-buried sand and gravel. That makes irrigation design less straightforward than the flat tracts further south: zoning has to account for grade changes, isolated wet spots over slow-draining clay, and the estate pasture or paddock setup that homeowners often want kept on a separate schedule from turf.

The wooded character of Barrington — particularly through North Barrington, Tower Lake, and the area around Cuba Marsh — shapes the rest of the work. Landscape lighting leans heavily on tree uplighting and pathlights woven through mature plantings rather than driveway-edge strips. Holiday lighting on the traditional New England-style and brick farmhouse homes in the village core is a substantial seasonal market. Brick and flagstone paver patios — many installed in the 1990s as part of the area's first wave of larger homebuilds — are now in the window where polymeric re-sanding and edge stabilization start to matter. We've worked across all of it for over 25 years.

cities.name: Crystal Lake
cities.intro:
Crystal Lake is the biggest market in McHenry County and the geology shifts noticeably as you move around the lake itself. Properties closest to the water — through the Crystal Lake South area, around the Park District beach, and into Lakewood — sit on glacial outwash sand that drains fast and dries out quickly under summer sun. Further from the lake, in the older Veterans Acres neighborhoods and out toward the Route 14 corridor, you're back to clay-loam with the slower drainage typical of inland McHenry County. That difference shows up in irrigation runtime and zone design: properties on sandy soil need more frequent, shorter cycles, while clay-loam yards do better with deep, infrequent watering.

Crystal Lake's housing stock is mostly 1980s through 2010s mid-size suburban — not the estate-heavy market of the North Shore. That shifts what most jobs look like: residential sprinkler systems on quarter- to half-acre lots, holiday lighting that emphasizes rooflines and front entries, and paver patios and driveway aprons rather than long bluestone walks. Landscape lighting still has real demand here, especially on properties backing wooded conservation easements where uplit specimen trees actually have a backdrop. We've been pulling permits in McHenry County since 2000.

cities.name: Highland Park
cities.intro:
Highland Park sits on the same Lake Michigan bluff system as Lake Forest, with the same ravine-shaped geology — clay-loam with pockets that drain east toward the lake — but a denser housing stock and slightly tighter lot pattern. The Ravinia and Briar Wood areas, and the bluff-edge properties along Sheridan Road, show up most often on our schedule. As in Lake Forest, mature oak and elm canopies set the conditions: heavy shade fights with turf irrigation goals, and zone design has to account for trees competing aggressively with shallow-rooted lawn for water through summer dry spells. Spring start-ups here run on the Lake Forest timeline — mid-to-late May — because of the same lake-cooled overnight temperatures.

What's distinct about Highland Park is the visibility of outdoor lighting in the Ravinia neighborhood: with the festival drawing thousands through the season, holiday lighting and architectural landscape lighting on the homes around Lake Cook Road and St. Johns Avenue get more attention than in less-trafficked corners of the North Shore. Brick and bluestone paver driveways are common throughout the older sections of town and are now well into the window where polymeric joint re-sanding and sealing matter most. We've worked across all of these conditions for over 25 years.

cities.name: Lake Forest
cities.intro:
Lake Forest sits along Lake Michigan's bluffs in central Lake County, where ravines drain east toward the lake and large lots are shaded by mature oaks, maples, and elms. That landscape sets the tone for outdoor work here. Sprinkler systems contend with heavy clay-loam soils and pockets of poor drainage near the ravine edges — proper zone design and head spacing matter more than they do in newer subdivisions. We typically delay spring start-ups in Lake Forest until mid-to-late May because the lake-cooled air can hold overnight frost a few weeks past the inland counties.

Estate properties around Onwentsia, Knollwood, and east of Sheridan Road are where landscape lighting carries the most weight: long driveways, gated entries, uplit specimen trees, and downlit terraces transform the night without overwhelming the architecture. Traditional holiday lighting on the Tudor and Georgian homes near Market Square is another seasonal staple. Bluestone walkways and clay-brick paver driveways — common throughout central Lake Forest — eventually need polymeric re-sanding and sealing, especially after the first decade of freeze–thaw. We've worked across all of these conditions for over 25 years.

cities.name: Lake Geneva
cities.intro:
Lake Geneva is the Wisconsin half of our service area, and the work here is shaped by both the lake itself and the area's status as a weekend-home community for Chicago and Milwaukee. Lakeshore properties — particularly the historic estates along the south shore through Williams Bay and Fontana, and the homes off the Geneva Lake Shore Path — sit on glacial outwash with a high water table close to the lake and granular soils that drain fast. That changes irrigation design: sprinkler heads need careful pressure regulation on grade, and we usually delay spring start-ups until late May because the lake holds cooler air longer than inland Wisconsin or Northern Illinois.

Because so many properties here are second homes, the irrigation, lighting, and paver work we do has to operate well even with the owner absent for stretches. Smart controllers, leak-detection flow sensors, and lighting on dusk-to-dawn timers become defaults rather than upgrades. Landscape lighting is a major piece of the Lake Geneva market — long lakefront sightlines, illuminated specimen trees, and lit paver paths down to the water. The historic Tudor and shingle-style homes around Geneva National and Linn Pier are also a stronghold for traditional holiday lighting. We carry the Wisconsin licensing required for irrigation work, including the mandatory RPZ backflow assembly.
